mappings.rationaleAI: Herodotus pioneered historical methodology, combining geography with ethnography.
Oracle bones, Dunhuang cache: primary sources, textual criticism, palaeography, East Asian historiography.
Plutarch's Parallel Lives: comparative biographical method, Enlightenment influence.
Thucydides: systematic objectivity, foundational historiography.
Gibbon's Decline and Fall: Enlightenment historiography.

mappings.episodeTitle: Tacitus and the Decadence of Rome
mappings.rationaleAI: Tacitus: Roman historiography combining moral judgment with political analysis.
Bede: medieval Christian historiography, Anno Domini chronology.
Josephus: Jewish-Roman historical accounts, vantage point shaping narrative.
Comprehensive survey of historiography from Thucydides to postmodernism.
Idea of progress: speculative philosophy of history.
Library of Alexandria: cataloguing, textual criticism, bibliography.

mappings.episodeTitle: Giorgio Vasari's Lives of the Artists
mappings.rationaleAI: Vasari: periodization, biographical method, founding art history.
20th-century debates over historiography as discipline.
Ashoka's edicts: epigraphy, chronology as auxiliary disciplines.
Writing technology from scrolls to printing: material sources.
Abbasid translation: Islamic historiographical traditions.
Al-Biruni's India: cross-cultural scholarly inquiry.
Writing systems from Mesopotamia: material sources for history.
Humanism: intellectual framework for Renaissance historiography.
Marx: materialist philosophy of history.
12th-century recovery of Aristotle: transformed conditions for historical writing.

Addresses historiographic framing of Africa by Western scholars (A.1.c.iv 19th- and 20th-century historiography) and philosophical questions about colonial frameworks of understanding as speculative philosophy of history (C).

The evolution of archaeology from adventurous antiquarianism to rigorous science is central to archaeology as an auxiliary discipline of historical investigation (B.2.b), and 19th-20th century historiographic developments shaped by nationalism (A.1.c.iv).

The episode addresses how biblical and classical sources gave Babylon an unfairly negative reputation, engaging Near Eastern historiography (A.1.a.i) and speculative philosophy of history regarding historiographic bias (C).
Gerald's chronicles rank among the most informative medieval source texts, representing Western Christian historiography in the Middle Ages (A.1.b.i).

The episode directly addresses the philosophy of history -- conceptions of historical knowledge (C.1), speculative philosophy about historical patterns and determinism vs. contingency (C.2), types of historical writing (A.2), and the sources and methods of historical investigation (B.1).
The episode engages contested decisions about what to remember or suppress, connecting to conceptions of the philosophy of history (C.1) and speculative philosophy about historical patterns (C.2).

Ancient historians both celebrated and condemned Spartacus, connecting to Classical historiography (A.1.a.ii); his ideological afterlife in revolutionary politics and cinema demonstrates how historical figures are reinterpreted through speculative philosophy of history (C.2).
Strabo's work combines geographical description with ethnographic observation and historical narrative, connecting to Classical Greek and Roman historiography (A.1.a.ii).

The episode examines medieval historiography (A.1.b.i -- Western Christian historiography) and how historical myths are constructed and transmitted, connecting to types of historical writing and factors in historical interpretation (A.2).

Procopius's account and the scholarly debate over demographic impact connect to Classical historiography (A.1.a.ii), early Christian historiography (A.1.a.iii), and the use of material remains including mass graves and ancient DNA as sources for historical writing (B.1).

The episode's focus on Burckhardt's historiographic framework and the debate over whether the Renaissance represents rupture or continuity connects to historiographic methodology (A.3) and the philosophy of history (A.4).
Thucydides' recording of the debate is a foundational work of classical historiography, demonstrating Greek methods of historical writing and political analysis (A.1.a.ii).

The Rosetta Stone's role in making ancient Egyptian written records readable transformed historical investigation, relating to sources for historical writing and the methodology of modern historical research (B.1).
Xenophon continued Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War and wrote diverse historical works; he is a significant figure in Classical Greek historiography (A.1.a.ii)
